Mostly yes — but with an honesty caveat first: on five of the eight questions you picked the option I'd marked "(Recommended)," so "deferring to my judgment" leaves those standing almost by definition. The real review is of the three you chose without a recommendation from me. Verdict: I kept six, revised two, and everything below is now committed and pushed (`46491ac`).

**What I kept, and why it survives scrutiny:**

- **Shape A with a B-ready schema** — still the right call. It defers the crowded "code graph for agents" fight until the human surface is proven, and it gives the project a salvage path: if the human view flops, the store still has value as agent context.
- **TypeScript end-to-end, Stop-hook-only capture, both annotation sources compared** — all still right. The view is the hard part and it's web-native; one turn = one event batch is the simplest thing that can work; and the annotation comparison is a free experiment since the `origin` field already exists.
- **Browser view** — right, with one note: the load-bearing part is *zero-action display* (it's already on screen when the turn ends), not the second monitor specifically. Worth a laptop-only answer eventually, but not a v1 concern.

**What I revised:**

1. **Study mode: "passive, measure first" → "passive-plus-one."** This was the one where you went against my recommendation, and I still think the recommendation was right — here's the actual argument. Your instinct (measure before building) is sound, but a purely passive v1 runs your one clean self-experiment on the exact variant the comprehension research predicts will fail. If nobody opens it, you can't tell whether the *idea* is dead or the *passive variant* is weak. The fix costs almost nothing: the claim-vs-change panel ("Claude said X; here's what actually changed") just renders two things v1 already computes — annotations and the structural delta. No quiz engine, no flashcards. Instrumentation stays hard scope, so your measure-first instinct is preserved.
2. **Dogfood: "this repo itself" → develop here, experiment on a real repo.** A map of a 15-file repo trivializes every hard problem — layout stability, overload, delta-radiating views — and planning-heavy sessions don't produce real waits. So: this repo is the dev test bed, but the one-week experiment runs on whatever's genuinely active that week (likely Synapse). The pollution worry is near zero since capture only appends to `.codemap/`.

**And since you deferred on the essay questions too, my answers, now recorded as decisions:**

- **First three seconds:** top strip is the agent's claim line + turn number + freshness stamp; then the claim-vs-change panel; then the glowing treemap. Three seconds should answer: *what does the agent say it did, and where did it actually land.*
- **The branch problem:** `.codemap/` is worktree-local and excluded via `.git/info/exclude` (so the observed repo stays completely untouched — no `.gitignore` edit, no committed noise). Every event carries `branch` + `commit`, and the view reconciles history from provenance. The research's "JSONL git-merges well" argument was over-weighted for a solo v1; it becomes relevant again if this ever goes multi-machine.
- **The kill criterion, pre-registered in CURRENT-TODOs before any code exists:** build v2 if the map is open during ≥50% of waits over 60 seconds *and* ≥3 journaled instances where it traceably shaped your next prompt or caught an agent mistake. High opens with zero influence → pivot to shape B. Opens collapse by mid-week → the phone won; shelve the wait-time thesis and write down why.

One meta-note: I logged in the session notes that batching four big forks per round outpaced your attention — that's on me, and it's recorded so future planning sessions ask fewer, slower questions. If any of my revisions rub you wrong, say so and we'll argue it properly this time.

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message: Good question — treemaps are genuinely unintuitive the first time. Let me look at exactly what's on your screen right now so I explain your actual tiles, not a generic diagram.

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message:
Here's the key that makes it click: **the shape of each tile means nothing — only its size does.** That's the thing tripping you up, and it trips up everyone the first time.

What you're looking at is a **treemap** of your `src/` folder. Every tile is one source file, and the reading is:

- **Area (width × height) = how many lines that file has.** That's the only spatial thing worth reading. On your screen right now the biggest tile is `derive.ts` (263 lines), next to it `extract.ts` (254), then `server.ts` (185). The two slivers along the bottom are your tiniest files, `decay.ts` (13 lines) and `jsonl.ts` (14).
- **Whether a tile is tall-and-thin or wide-and-short carries no meaning at all.** A 49×175 tile and a 175×49 tile are the same-size file. Those proportions are just the algorithm packing rectangles to fill the box with no gaps — like fitting books of different thicknesses onto a shelf. Don't read anything into the direction; read the *bulk*.

So the honest mental translation of the whole picture is just: *"big blob = big file, little blob = little file, and they're packed to fill the space."*

Two more things are encoded, both in **color, not shape:**

- **Brightness = how recently the file was touched.** The brightest-blue tiles (`derive.ts`, `server.ts`, `hierarchy.ts`, `decay.ts`) are the ones changed *this turn* — that's the whole point of the tool, your eye getting pulled to what just moved. The dimmer, greyer-blue ones (`events.ts`, `git.ts`, `paths.ts`) were last touched several turns ago and are fading out. Over 8 turns an untouched file decays back to neutral.
- **Grouping = folders.** Tiles from the same directory sit together as a cluster, so `src/view/*` files are packed near each other, `src/capture/*` near each other, and so on. (Right now it's a bit hard to see the folder boundaries because this demo data doesn't draw the group outlines prominently — that'd show more on a bigger repo.)

And one property you can't see in a single screenshot but matters: **positions are stable.** A file keeps its spot turn after turn, even as its neighbors change size, so you can build muscle memory for "auth stuff lives top-left." That stability is a deliberate design rule, not an accident.

One caveat about *this particular* view: it's demo data with 18 files, all roughly the same size, so the treemap looks like a fairly uniform grid and isn't very striking. On a real repo — where a 2,000-line file sits next to a dozen 40-line ones — the size differences become dramatic and the map actually tells you "here's where the mass of this codebase is." The current picture is more "proof it renders" than "insight."

Want me to point it at a real repo (say Synapse) for a turn or two so you can see the treemap with meaningful size variation and real folder clusters? That's the version where the shape of the thing starts paying off.

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message:
Not GitHub — **git worktrees** are a plain-git feature, and honestly they're one of git's more obscure corners, so the confusion is fair.

**What a worktree is.** Normally one cloned repo means one folder with one branch checked out at a time. If you're on `main` and want to work on a feature branch, you switch, and the files in that one folder change under you. `git worktree` breaks that one-to-one rule: it lets a single repository have *several* working folders checked out at once, each on its own branch, all sharing the same underlying `.git` history database. So you could have `~/project/main/` on `main` and `~/project/feature-x/` on a feature branch, both real folders sitting side by side, no stashing or switching to bounce between them. Same repo, multiple live checkouts. Nothing to do with GitHub — it works on any local git repo.

**Where llm-dev comes in.** When llm-dev creates a *brand-new* project with its "container layout," it uses exactly this. Each **stream** (its name for a persistent line of work that sessions claim and hand off) gets its own worktree — its own folder on its own branch. `streams/main/` is one worktree; a second stream would be another folder next to it. So in llm-dev's model, one stream = one worktree = one working directory.

**Why that matters for codemap.** codemap writes its store (`.codemap/events.jsonl`) *inside the folder it's pointed at* — it's per-directory by design. I made it that way for a boring git-safety reason (a per-folder log avoids merge conflicts across branches). But if each llm-dev stream is already its own separate folder, then each stream automatically gets its own separate map, for free, without codemap needing to understand what a "stream" even is. That's what I meant by "fell out by accident" — two independent design choices happen to line up so that parallel streams get parallel maps with zero extra plumbing. That's the whole point of the bullet in the issue.

**One honest caveat** so you're not confused looking at your own repo: *this* project — live-codebase-map — does **not** use worktrees. Because the folder already existed when we ran llm-dev's init, it used the older flat layout: one folder, one branch, `.archive/` as an ordinary subdirectory (that's why every commit we've made just went straight to `main`). The worktree alignment is a property of how llm-dev structures *new* container-layout projects, not something visible in this repo. So it's a genuine architectural fit I'm pointing Dallas at — but you won't see it by poking around live-codebase-map itself.

If it'd help, I can soften that bullet in the draft so it reads clearly to Dallas as "here's a nice alignment for container-layout projects" rather than implying every llm-dev project already works this way — some don't.

dialogue.speaker: user
dialogue.message:
I think now I'm just wondering what you mean by code map. Is that what we've decided to call the encoding of a codebase's architecture and how we store it? Does that mean it's stored in every directory inside of a project or just the root of a project?

I'm asking because, at all times, regardless of the branch that I have, I want this architecture to be accurate to what is reflected on that branch. When these architectures are merged together, say we have two or three branches merging back into main, it should reflect whatever the state of main is after that.

I'm also wondering what that means. If you asked for the code map of the lowest, like the childmost, directory, if it has its own map, can you just render that? How do these code maps talk to each other? These questions are all under the assumption that code maps do propagate into every directory inside of a project rather than just existing at the root, but I think I just need some clarification on what code maps are.

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message: Good questions, and a couple of them poke at exactly the spot where what we *built* differs from what you're *imagining* — so let me check the actual code before I answer, rather than describe an aspiration as if it were real.

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message:
Verified against the actual code, so here's the straight version — and one of your assumptions is worth correcting up front.

**What "codemap" is.** Two things wearing one name: it's the project/tool (the CLI is literally `codemap`), and informally "the map" is the thing it produces — an **event store** (the append-only JSONL log of structural facts) plus the **renderings** of it (treemap, graph). So "codemap" = the encoding of the architecture *and* the store *and* the views, loosely. The encoding itself is the event log; everything else is derived from it.

**Where it's stored — and here's the correction: one store at the root, not per-directory.** I just confirmed it on HyprMac: there's a single `.codemap/` at the repo root (`events.jsonl` + `state.json`), and `find` shows no `.codemap` anywhere in the subdirectories. Your whole set of questions assumed maps propagate into every directory — they don't. There is one flat log for the entire repo. Directories aren't stored at all; the folder structure you see in the treemap is *reconstructed at view time* from the file paths in the events (`src/view/derive.ts` builds the hierarchy from path strings). So a directory has no map of its own sitting on disk — it has a *slice* of the one map, computed on demand.

That actually dissolves your "how do the code maps talk to each other" question: they don't need to, because there's only one graph. An import from `src/a/foo.ts` into `src/b/bar.ts` is just an edge in that single graph. If we *had* gone per-directory, that cross-directory edge would be the hard problem — which store owns it? how do they sync? — and you'd need a whole composition protocol. The single-store design sidesteps it entirely. And "render just the childmost directory" becomes a filter, not a lookup: because every entity carries its path, showing only `src/view/**` is a one-line query over the same log. Very doable; it's just not wired to a button yet.

Now the part you care most about — **"regardless of branch, I want the architecture accurate to that branch."** This is where I have to be honest: **that's a real requirement we do not currently satisfy, and it needs deliberate work.** Here's the actual behavior today:

- `.codemap/` is git-*excluded* (in `.git/info/exclude`), so it does **not** switch, stash, or travel when you change branches. It's a single local log that just keeps accumulating.
- Every event is *tagged* with its `branch` and `commit` — but I checked `derive.ts`, and the view **replays all events regardless of current branch**. Branch is stored as provenance; it is not a filter.
- Capture only fires on an agent turn. Switching branches by hand doesn't re-capture (the file watcher was deferred), so the map can be stale relative to whatever's actually checked out.

So right now, on a given branch, you're looking at an *accumulation of everything that's happened in that working directory*, not a clean picture of that branch. To get the accurate-to-this-branch behavior you're describing, the reliable primitive is **`codemap scan`** — it ignores history and re-derives the map from the actual file contents currently on disk, so it always reflects exactly what's checked out right now. The productized version of your requirement would be an automatic scan on branch switch (a git `post-checkout` hook), plus optionally teaching the view to filter the log to the current branch. Neither is built; both are straightforward. Branch-accurate replay from the log *alone* (without a scan) is the harder, later version.

**Merges follow from the same fact.** Because the store is git-excluded, when you merge branch-X into `main`, the *code* merges but `.codemap/` does not — git never touches it. So the log won't magically "reflect main after the merge." The honest, working answer is: **run `codemap scan` on main after the merge**, and the map is exactly correct, because scan reads the merged files directly. The alternative — committing the log so it genuinely git-merges with the code and reflects merged main automatically — is appealing and is exactly what the research floated, but we *deliberately deferred* it (it reintroduces merge conflicts in the log and pollutes history). For now: **the log is history; `scan` is ground truth.**

If I had to compress all of it into one mental model: **storage is flat and singular (one log at the root, tagged by branch/commit); the map is a *view* computed from it, and views can be scoped any way you like — to a subtree, to a branch, to a range of turns.** Directories and branches don't own separate maps; they own separate *queries* against the one store. Your instinct toward branch-accuracy and directory-scoping is good and the data model can support both — they're view/refresh features we haven't built, not things fighting the architecture.

Want me to write these up as backlog items (auto-scan on checkout for branch-accuracy; scoped/subtree view for the childmost-directory case) so they're captured before they evaporate? That's the natural home for them, and it keeps the issue-to-Dallas focused on the thesis rather than these mechanics.

dialogue.speaker: user
dialogue.message: Okay, I understand, and I think we're mostly going in the right direction with having one code map per project. However, is there any reason that we are git excluding the code map folder at the root? If we included it in the repository, would it not automatically take on the behavior that I described, where the architecture is accurate to that branch?

dialogue.speaker: assistant
dialogue.message:
There's a reason, but it's a circumstantial one, not a law — and your instinct about what committing would buy is mostly correct. Let me give you the honest trade-off, because I made that call back when the scope was "solo dogfood on one machine," and you're now describing exactly the scenario (branches, merges, shared truth) where the calculus changes.

**Why it's excluded today:** three small reasons. The log churns every single agent turn, so committing it means every commit drags map noise into diffs and `git status` is perpetually dirty. Naive git also *conflicts* on append-only files — two branches both appending to the tail of `events.jsonl` is the textbook "both sides modified the same region" conflict, so "JSONL merges fine" isn't true out of the box. And for a v1 whose only user was you-on-one-machine, exclusion made the whole question disappear.

**What committing it would genuinely buy you:** branch-travel, exactly as you intuit. Check out `feature-x`, and the log — and therefore the derived map — is whatever that branch last committed. The map's history becomes shared: a collaborator who clones gets the annotations and summaries, which are the irreplaceable part (structure can always be re-scanned from code; the accumulated "why" layer can't). That's a real, meaningful upgrade, and the mechanical objections have standard fixes: a `.gitattributes` line marking `events.jsonl` with the **union merge driver** makes branch merges concatenate both sides' events instead of conflicting (the derive layer already orders by timestamp, so interleaving is harmless), and `linguist-generated` collapses it in PR diffs so reviewers never see the churn.

**The one place your expectation needs trimming:** a merged log still doesn't *guarantee* "accurate to main after the merge." The union-merged log knows what each branch observed, but it knows nothing about how you resolved code conflicts during the merge — if both branches touched `TilingEngine.swift` and you hand-resolved, the log's last-writer view of that file may match neither side's final reality. So `codemap scan` remains the ground-truth refresher after a merge. The difference committing makes is that the post-merge scan becomes a *small corrective delta* appended on top of a mostly-right log, instead of the log being unrelated to branch state entirely. (And that scan could be automated with a git `post-merge` hook, at which point your described behavior — merge three branches, map reflects the result — is effectively real.)

**Two things should stay uncommitted no matter what.** `state.json` is a derived cache that changes wholesale every turn — committing it would conflict constantly, and it's rebuildable from the log, so it stays local. And `telemetry.jsonl` is your personal behavioral data (opens, dwell time); that has no business in a repo, especially a public one. Related privacy point worth saying out loud: `events.jsonl` contains harvested agent turn-text and `MAP:` notes — committing it to a *public* repo publishes your agent's narration of your work. Fine for some repos, worth a conscious choice per repo.

So my actual position: you've talked me into it as the direction, with the design being **commit `events.jsonl` (with union merge + linguist-generated attributes), keep `state.json` and `telemetry.jsonl` local, and pair it with scan-on-merge/checkout for ground truth**. It probably lands as a per-repo choice at `codemap init` time (`--tracked` vs `--local`), because the privacy trade-off is real and repo-specific. The dogfood-honest way to validate it is to flip this very repo's own store to tracked mode and live with the diff noise for a week.

Want me to implement tracked mode and flip live-codebase-map's store over? It's a contained change (init flag, gitattributes, scan hooks), and it would also resolve the merge question in the Dallas issue draft from "deferred" to "designed, being validated" — a stronger position to propose from. Or if you'd rather keep momentum on the issue itself, this holds as a recorded decision until after Dallas weighs in.
